title: Multiple recurring tasks repeat every a, b, c days; find the first day they coincide again, and how many coincidences occur in N days.
description: Multiple recurring tasks repeat every a, b, c days; find the first day they coincide again, and how many coincidences occur in N days.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(N * k)
approaches.summary: Iterate through each day from 1 to N, checking if the day is divisible by all given periods (a, b, c, etc.) to find coincidences. This directly simulates the problem but is inefficient for large N or many periods.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(N * k), where k is the number of periods (e.g., a, b, c)

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Multiple Task Coincidence Finder
ladders.desc: Given multiple recurring tasks with periods a, b, c..., find the first day they all coincide again and count how many coincidences occur within N days using brute force iteration.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding days when multiple periodic tasks align. The brute force approach directly simulates the problem by checking each day from 1 to N. For each day, we verify if it's divisible by all given periods (a, b, c...). If divisible by all, it's a coincidence day. We track the first such day (if any) and count all coincidences. The intuition is straightforward: tasks coincide when the day number is a common multiple of all periods. By checking every day, we're guaranteed to find all coincidences, though inefficiently. The algorithm: 1) Initialize first_coincidence = -1 (not found) and count = 0. 2) Loop day from 1 to N inclusive. 3) For each day, check if day % a == 0 AND day % b == 0 AND day % c == 0... for all periods. 4) If true: if first_coincidence == -1, set first_coincidence = day; increment count. 5) After loop, return (first_coincidence, count). Time complexity is O(N * k) where k is number of periods, as we perform up to k modulo operations per day. Space is O(1) using only a few variables. This connects to the parent problem by providing a complete, albeit slow, solution. Common mistakes include: starting loop at 0 (tasks don't coincide on day 0), forgetting to handle case with no coincidences (return -1 for first), or using incorrect modulo logic. Real code pattern: def find_coincidences(periods, N): first = -1; count = 0; for day in range(1, N+1): if all(day % p == 0 for p in periods): if first == -1: first = day; count += 1; return (first, count).

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Checking Day Divisibility for Multiple Periods
ladders.desc: Given a day number and a list of periods, determine if the day is divisible by all periods (i.e., a common multiple).
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core check in the brute force approach. For a given day, we need to verify if it's divisible by each period a, b, c... meaning day % period == 0 for all periods. The intuition: a task with period p occurs on days that are multiples of p. For multiple tasks to coincide, the day must be a multiple of all periods simultaneously—a common multiple. The algorithm: 1) Input: day (integer), periods (list of integers). 2) For each period p in periods: if day % p != 0, return False. 3) If all checks pass, return True. Time complexity is O(k) where k is number of periods, as we perform up to k modulo operations. Space is O(1). This feeds into L0 by being called for each day in the main loop. Common mistakes: using division instead of modulo, not handling edge cases like period=0 (invalid here), or short-circuiting incorrectly. Real code pattern: def is_coincidence_day(day, periods): for p in periods: if day % p != 0: return False; return True. This can be optimized with Python's all() function: all(day % p == 0 for p in periods).

examples.trace: Check period 3: 12 % 3 = 0 → continue. Check period 4: 12 % 4 = 0 → all passed, return True.
Check period 3: 15 % 3 = 0 → continue. Check period 5: 15 % 5 = 0 → continue. Check period 6: 15 % 6 = 3 ≠ 0 → return False.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterating Days and Counting Coincidences
ladders.desc: Loop through days 1 to N, using the divisibility check to find and count coincidence days.
ladders.explanation: This level implements the main loop structure of the brute force approach. We iterate through each possible day from 1 to N, apply the divisibility check (from L1), and track results. The intuition: since tasks repeat periodically, coincidences can only occur on days that are common multiples of the periods. By checking every day in range, we exhaustively find all such days. The algorithm: 1) Initialize first = -1 (indicating not found) and count = 0. 2) For day in 1 to N inclusive: if is_coincidence_day(day, periods) is True: if first == -1, set first = day; increment count by 1. 3) After loop, return (first, count). Time complexity is O(N * k) due to N iterations each calling O(k) check. Space is O(1). This is the central technique in L0, combining iteration with the check. Common mistakes: starting loop at 0 (day 0 isn't valid for task occurrence), forgetting to update first only on first coincidence, or not handling N=0 correctly. Real code pattern: def find_coincidences(periods, N): first = -1; count = 0; for day in range(1, N+1): if all(day % p == 0 for p in periods): if first == -1: first = day; count += 1; return (first, count).

examples.trace: Day 1: not coincidence. Day 2: not. Day 3: not. Day 4: not. Day 5: not. Day 6: coincidence → first=6, count=1. Day 7: not. Day 8: not. Day 9: not. Day 10: not. Day 11: not. Day 12: coincidence → count=2. Final: (6, 2).

ladders.title: Building Block — Finding First Coincidence Day in a Range
ladders.desc: Given periods and a maximum day N, find the smallest day ≤ N where all tasks coincide (or -1 if none).
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept focuses on finding just the first coincidence day, without counting all. It's a simplification of L2. The intuition: the first coincidence is the least common multiple (LCM) of the periods, but here we find it via brute force by checking days in order until we hit a coincidence. The algorithm: 1) For day from 1 to N: if day is divisible by all periods (using modulo checks), return day. 2) If loop ends without finding, return -1. Time complexity O(N * k), space O(1). This feeds into L2 by being part of the tracking logic. Common mistakes: not checking days in ascending order, returning 0 for no coincidence (should be -1 or similar sentinel), or incorrect modulo logic. Real code pattern: def first_coincidence(periods, N): for day in range(1, N+1): if all(day % p == 0 for p in periods): return day; return -1.

examples.trace: Day 1-15: Check each: Day 4: 4%4=0, 4%10=4 → no. Day 8: 8%4=0, 8%10=8 → no. Day 12: 12%4=0, 12%10=2 → no. Day 16-20: Day 20: 20%4=0, 20%10=0 → yes, return 20. (Note: N=15, but we check up to 20? Wait, error in trace—should be N=20. Let's correct: Input N=20, trace days 1-20: Day 20 is first, output 20.)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Checking Divisibility of a Number by Another
ladders.desc: Given two integers, determine if one is divisible by the other using the modulo operator.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation checks if a number 'day' is divisible by a period 'p', i.e., if day is a multiple of p. The intuition: division results in no remainder. We use the modulo operator (%) which returns the remainder of division. If day % p == 0, then day is divisible by p. The algorithm: 1) Compute remainder = day % p. 2) If remainder == 0, return True; else False. Time and space are O(1). This is the building block for L3 and above, as each coincidence check relies on multiple such divisibility checks. Common mistakes: confusing modulo with integer division, not handling p=0 (division by zero, but periods are positive here), or using floating-point division. Real code pattern: def is_divisible(day, p): return day % p == 0.

examples.trace: Compute 15 % 3: 15 divided by 3 is 5 with remainder 0. Since remainder 0 == 0, return True.
Compute 17 % 5: 17 divided by 5 is 3 with remainder 2. Since remainder 2 != 0, return False.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Looping Through a Range of Numbers
ladders.desc: Iterate through integers from a start to an end value, performing an operation on each.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: understanding how to loop through a sequence of numbers. In programming, we often use for-loops or while-loops to iterate. For this problem, we need to check each day from 1 to N. The intuition: to examine all possible days, we systematically go through them one by one. The algorithm: 1) Set a variable (e.g., day) to start value (1). 2) While day ≤ end value (N): perform operation (e.g., check divisibility), then increment day by 1. Alternatively, use a for-loop: for day in range(start, end+1): ... Time complexity depends on operations inside loop; here it's linear in N. Space is O(1). This foundation connects to L4 and above by providing the iteration structure. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors (e.g., using < instead of ≤), infinite loops if increment is missing, or misunderstanding loop bounds. Real code pattern: for day in range(1, N+1): # do something with day.

examples.trace: Initialize day=1. Check 1 ≤ 5: yes, print 1, set day=2. Check 2 ≤ 5: yes, print 2, set day=3. Check 3 ≤ 5: yes, print 3, set day=4. Check 4 ≤ 5: yes, print 4, set day=5. Check 5 ≤ 5: yes, print 5, set day=6. Check 6 ≤ 5: no, stop.

Initialize sum=0, day=3. Check 3 ≤ 7: yes, sum=0+3=3, day=4. Check 4 ≤ 7: yes, sum=3+4=7, day=5. Check 5 ≤ 7: yes, sum=7+5=12, day=6. Check 6 ≤ 7: yes, sum=12+6=18, day=7. Check 7 ≤ 7: yes, sum=18+7=25, day=8. Check 8 ≤ 7: no, stop, output sum=25.

testCases.input: start = 1, end = 3, operation = return list of days
start = 2, end = 6, operation = check if day even
start = 3, end = 8, operation = find max day
start = 6, end = 9, operation = list in reverse
start = 1, end = 100, operation = number of iterations

approaches.name: LCM-Based O(N / L)
approaches.summary: Compute the least common multiple (LCM) of all periods to find the first coincidence day, then count coincidences by iterating through multiples of the LCM up to N. This reduces the search space significantly compared to brute force.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(N / L + log(max period)), where L is the LCM of periods

ladders.title: Full Problem — Multiple Recurring Tasks Coincidence Finder
ladders.desc: Given task periods a, b, c and total days N, find first coincidence day and count of coincidences up to N.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding when multiple periodic events align. The key insight is that tasks coincide when the day number is a common multiple of all periods. The first coincidence occurs at the least common multiple (LCM) of a, b, and c. Subsequent coincidences happen at multiples of this LCM. To solve: 1) Compute LCM of all periods using the relationship LCM(a,b) = a*b/GCD(a,b). 2) The first coincidence day is this LCM (if ≤ N, else no coincidence). 3) Count coincidences by dividing N by LCM (integer division). Time complexity is O(N/L + log(max period)) where L is LCM, dominated by counting multiples and GCD computations. Space is O(1). The approach reduces from brute force O(N) to efficient mathematical computation. Connection: This integrates LCM computation and counting logic. Common mistakes: forgetting to handle cases where LCM exceeds N (return 0), integer overflow with large periods (use long integers), and incorrect LCM computation order (LCM(a,b,c) = LCM(LCM(a,b),c)). Real pattern: compute GCD via Euclidean algorithm, then LCM sequentially.

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute LCM of 2,3,4. GCD(2,3)=1, LCM(2,3)=2*3/1=6. GCD(6,4)=2, LCM(6,4)=6*4/2=12. Step 2: First coincidence day = 12. Step 3: Count coincidences = floor(20/12) = 1. Coincidence days: 12.
Step 1: LCM of 5,10,15. GCD(5,10)=5, LCM(5,10)=5*10/5=10. GCD(10,15)=5, LCM(10,15)=10*15/5=30. Step 2: First coincidence day = 30. Step 3: Count = floor(100/30) = 3. Coincidence days: 30, 60, 90.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — LCM Computation for Multiple Numbers
ladders.desc: Compute the least common multiple of three integers a, b, c.
ladders.explanation: The LCM is the smallest positive integer divisible by all given numbers. For multiple numbers, LCM can be computed sequentially: LCM(a,b,c) = LCM(LCM(a,b), c). The core operation uses the relationship between LCM and GCD: LCM(x,y) = x*y / GCD(x,y). This works because any common multiple must contain all prime factors of each number, and LCM is the product with shared factors counted once (handled by GCD). Steps: 1) Compute GCD of first two numbers using Euclidean algorithm. 2) Compute LCM of first two as (a*b)/GCD(a,b). 3) Repeat with result and next number. Time complexity is O(log(max(a,b,c))) per GCD computation. Space is O(1). Connection to L0: This LCM value directly gives the coincidence period. Common mistakes: integer overflow in multiplication (use integer division first: a/GCD * b), incorrect GCD for zero (handle separately), and not extending to more numbers. Real pattern: iterative LCM accumulation.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Finding Coincidence Days via LCM Multiples
ladders.desc: Given LCM L and days N, find first coincidence day and count of multiples of L ≤ N.
ladders.explanation: Once LCM L is known, all coincidence days are exactly the multiples of L: L, 2L, 3L, ... up to N. This works because L is the smallest number divisible by all periods, so any common multiple must be a multiple of L. The first coincidence day is L itself (if L ≤ N). The count of coincidences is the number of multiples of L within N, computed as floor(N/L). Steps: 1) If L > N, first coincidence is L but count is 0 (no coincidences within N). 2) Otherwise, first = L, count = N // L. Time O(1), space O(1). Connection to L1: Uses LCM result. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors (use integer division, not ceiling), missing case when L > N, and confusion between first coincidence day (always L) vs. first within N (L if L≤N). Real pattern: simple division after LCM.

examples.trace: Step 1: L=12 ≤ N=50, so first coincidence day = 12. Step 2: Count = floor(50/12) = 4. Multiples: 12, 24, 36, 48.
Step 1: L=100 > N=80, so no coincidences within N. First coincidence day is 100 (but outside range). Count = floor(80/100) = 0.

ladders.title: Building Block — Computing LCM of Two Numbers
ladders.desc: Calculate the least common multiple of two integers using their GCD.
ladders.explanation: The LCM of two numbers a and b is the smallest positive integer divisible by both. It can be efficiently computed using the formula: LCM(a,b) = a * b / GCD(a,b). This works because GCD represents the shared factors, and dividing removes duplication. Steps: 1) Compute GCD(a,b) using Euclidean algorithm. 2) Compute LCM = (a / GCD) * b to avoid overflow. Time complexity is O(log(min(a,b))) for GCD. Space O(1). Connection to L2: This is the fundamental pairwise operation for multi-number LCM. Common mistakes: integer overflow (compute as (a/GCD)*b), division by zero (handle a or b = 0 separately, LCM(0,x)=0), and incorrect GCD implementation. Real pattern: LCM function calling GCD.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Euclidean Algorithm for GCD
ladders.desc: Compute the greatest common divisor of two integers using the Euclidean algorithm.
ladders.explanation: The GCD of two numbers is the largest integer that divides both without remainder. The Euclidean algorithm is based on the property: GCD(a,b) = GCD(b, a mod b). It repeatedly replaces the larger number with the remainder until the remainder is 0, at which point the other number is the GCD. Steps: 1) While b ≠ 0: set a, b = b, a % b. 2) Return a. Time complexity is O(log(min(a,b))) as numbers reduce quickly. Space O(1). Connection to L3: GCD is needed for LCM computation. Common mistakes: not handling negative numbers (use absolute values), infinite loop if not updating variables correctly, and edge case when one number is 0 (GCD(a,0)=|a|). Real pattern: iterative modulo reduction.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Multiples and Divisibility
ladders.desc: Understand multiples of a number and divisibility rules.
ladders.explanation: A multiple of an integer a is any number that can be expressed as a * k where k is an integer. For example, multiples of 3 are 3, 6, 9, 12, ... Divisibility means one number divides another without remainder: a divides b if b % a == 0. Key concepts: 1) Common multiples of two numbers are multiples shared by both (e.g., common multiples of 2 and 3 include 6, 12, 18). 2) The least common multiple (LCM) is the smallest positive common multiple. 3) If d divides both a and b, then d divides any linear combination. This foundation explains why coinciding tasks occur at common multiples. Connection to L4: GCD is related to shared divisors. Common mistakes: confusing multiples with factors, misapplying divisibility to non-integers, and not recognizing that 0 is a multiple of every number (0 = a*0). Real pattern: iterating k from 1 to generate multiples.

examples.input: a=5, b=6, find first common multiple
examples.trace: Multiples of 5: 5,10,15,20,25,30,... Multiples of 6: 6,12,18,24,30,... First common is 30.

approaches.name: Direct LCM Formula O(log(max period))
approaches.summary: Calculate the LCM using the formula LCM(a,b) = a*b / GCD(a,b), extended to multiple periods, then compute the first coincidence as the LCM and the count as floor(N / LCM). This provides a closed-form solution without iteration.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(k * log(max period)), where k is the number of periods

ladders.title: Full Problem — Multiple Recurring Tasks Coincidence
ladders.desc: Given multiple tasks repeating every a, b, c days, find the first day they coincide again and how many coincidences occur in N days.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding when multiple periodic events align. The key insight is that tasks coincide on days that are common multiples of all their periods. The first coincidence after day 0 is the Least Common Multiple (LCM) of all periods. To find how many coincidences occur within N days, we count how many multiples of this LCM fit into N, which is floor(N/LCM). For multiple periods, we compute LCM iteratively: LCM(a,b,c) = LCM(LCM(a,b), c). The formula LCM(x,y) = x*y/GCD(x,y) provides an efficient O(log(max period)) method using Euclid's algorithm for GCD. The algorithm: 1) Initialize result = first period. 2) For each subsequent period, compute result = LCM(result, current_period). 3) First coincidence = result. 4) Count = floor(N/result). Time complexity is O(k * log(max period)) where k is number of periods, space is O(1). Common mistakes: forgetting that day 0 counts as a coincidence (so first coincidence after day 0 is LCM, not 0), integer overflow when multiplying large numbers before dividing by GCD (use result/GCD * current_period pattern), and misunderstanding that LCM of 0 is undefined. The connection to L1 is that this full solution relies on the LCM computation subroutine.

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute LCM of all periods. Start with result = 3. Compute LCM(3,4): GCD(3,4)=1, LCM=3*4/1=12. Update result=12. Compute LCM(12,6): GCD(12,6)=6, LCM=12*6/6=12. Final LCM=12. Step 2: First coincidence day = 12. Step 3: Count coincidences in 30 days: floor(30/12)=2. Coincidence days: 0, 12, 24. So first after day 0 is 12, total 3 coincidences including day 0, but problem asks for first coincidence again (after day 0) and count within N days including day 0? Typically 'first day they coincide again' means after day 0, so 12. Count usually includes day 0, so floor(N/LCM)+1? Let's clarify: The description says 'find the first day they coincide again' implying after initial coincidence at day 0, so first again is LCM. 'how many coincidences occur in N days' - if N=30, coincidences at days 0,12,24 = 3. But common interpretation: first coincidence after day 0 = LCM, count of coincidences from day 0 to N inclusive = floor(N/LCM)+1. However, many problems define count as number of multiples of LCM ≤ N, which is floor(N/LCM) if starting from day 0? Actually multiples: 0, LCM, 2*LCM,... ≤ N. Number = floor(N/LCM)+1. For N=30, LCM=12: multiples: 0,12,24 → 3. floor(30/12)=2, plus 1 =3. But the example output says count=2, so perhaps they exclude day 0. Let's re-read: 'find the first day they coincide again' - again suggests after the initial time, so day 0 is the first coincidence, we want the next one. 'how many coincidences occur in N days' - if N=30, and we start counting from day 1? Or including day 0? The output count=2 suggests coincidences at day 12 and 24 only, excluding day 0. So we'll adopt: first coincidence after day 0 = LCM, count of coincidences after day 0 up to N = floor(N/LCM). For N=30, floor(30/12)=2. So output: first=12, count=2.

Step 1: Compute LCM. Start result=5. LCM(5,7): GCD(5,7)=1, LCM=5*7/1=35. Update result=35. LCM(35,8): GCD(35,8)=1, LCM=35*8/1=280. Final LCM=280. Step 2: First coincidence after day 0 = 280. Step 3: Count coincidences after day 0 up to N=100: floor(100/280)=0. So output: first=280, count=0.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compute LCM of Multiple Numbers
ladders.desc: Given a list of integers, compute their Least Common Multiple using iterative LCM formula.
ladders.explanation: The LCM of multiple numbers is the smallest positive integer divisible by all of them. To compute LCM(a,b,c,...), we can iteratively apply the binary LCM operation: start with the first number, then repeatedly compute LCM(current_result, next_number). The formula LCM(x,y) = x*y / GCD(x,y) allows efficient computation using the Euclidean algorithm for GCD. The intuition: LCM accumulates the minimal factors needed to cover all numbers. For example, LCM(4,6)=12 because prime factors: 4=2², 6=2*3, LCM needs 2²*3=12. The iterative approach works because LCM is associative: LCM(a,b,c) = LCM(LCM(a,b), c). Algorithm: 1) Initialize result = first number. 2) For each subsequent number num in list: result = lcm(result, num). 3) Return result. The lcm function: compute gcd using Euclidean algorithm, then return a*b/gcd. Time complexity: O(k * log(max value)) for k numbers, as each gcd takes O(log(max)). Space: O(1). Common mistakes: integer overflow when multiplying large numbers (compute as result/gcd * num to minimize overflow), handling zeros (LCM with 0 is undefined, so ensure all numbers >0), and forgetting that LCM of a single number is the number itself. This sub-routine feeds into L0 by providing the core LCM computation needed to find the coincidence period.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize result = 3. Step 2: Process 4: Compute LCM(3,4). GCD(3,4): 4 % 3 = 1, 3 % 1 = 0, so GCD=1. LCM = 3*4/1 = 12. Update result = 12. Step 3: Process 6: Compute LCM(12,6). GCD(12,6): 12 % 6 = 0, so GCD=6. LCM = 12*6/6 = 12. Update result = 12. Final result = 12.

ladders.title: Core Logic — LCM Formula Using GCD
ladders.desc: Compute Least Common Multiple of two numbers using LCM(a,b) = a*b / GCD(a,b).
ladders.explanation: The Least Common Multiple (LCM) of two integers a and b is the smallest positive integer divisible by both a and b. The formula LCM(a,b) = a*b / GCD(a,b) provides an efficient way to compute LCM without prime factorization. The intuition: The product a*b contains all prime factors from both numbers, but the GCD represents the common factors that are counted twice. Dividing by GCD removes this duplication, leaving the minimal combination of factors. For example, a=12 (2²*3), b=18 (2*3²). GCD=6 (2*3), product=216, LCM=216/6=36, which matches 2²*3². The algorithm: 1) Compute GCD of a and b using Euclidean algorithm. 2) Compute LCM = a*b / GCD. Note: to avoid integer overflow for large numbers, compute as (a / GCD) * b. Time complexity: O(log(min(a,b))) for GCD computation, so overall O(log(min(a,b))). Space: O(1). Common mistakes: forgetting to ensure integers are positive, division before multiplication causing floating-point issues (use integer division), and not handling zero correctly (LCM(0,b) is undefined if b≠0, but often defined as 0 in some contexts; here we assume positive inputs). This core logic is used in L1's iterative LCM computation for multiple numbers.

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute GCD(12,18). Euclidean algorithm: 18 % 12 = 6, 12 % 6 = 0, so GCD=6. Step 2: LCM = 12 * 18 / 6 = 216 / 6 = 36. Alternatively, (12/6)*18 = 2*18=36.

ladders.title: Building Block — Euclidean Algorithm for GCD
ladders.desc: Compute Greatest Common Divisor of two numbers using the Euclidean algorithm.
ladders.explanation: The Greatest Common Divisor (GCD) of two integers a and b is the largest positive integer that divides both a and b without remainder. The Euclidean algorithm is based on the principle that GCD(a,b) = GCD(b, a mod b). The intuition: If d divides both a and b, then it also divides a - b, and by extension a mod b (remainder when a divided by b). By repeatedly replacing the larger number with the remainder, we reduce the numbers until the remainder is 0, at which point the other number is the GCD. Algorithm: 1) While b ≠ 0: set temp = b, b = a mod b, a = temp. 2) Return a. Alternatively, using recursion: if b==0 return a else return gcd(b, a mod b). Time complexity: O(log(min(a,b))) because the numbers reduce quickly (at least by factor ~1.618 each step, similar to Fibonacci). Space: O(1) for iterative version, O(log n) for recursion due to call stack. Common mistakes: not handling negative numbers (take absolute values), infinite loop if not updating variables correctly, and confusion with modulus operation for negative numbers (use abs or ensure positive inputs). This building block is essential for L2's LCM formula, as GCD is needed to compute LCM efficiently.

examples.trace: Step 1: a=48, b=18. Compute 48 mod 18 = 12. Update: a=18, b=12. Step 2: 18 mod 12 = 6. Update: a=12, b=6. Step 3: 12 mod 6 = 0. Update: a=6, b=0. Since b=0, GCD = a = 6.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Modulo and Integer Division
ladders.desc: Perform modulo (remainder) and integer division operations on integers.
ladders.explanation: Modulo operation (a % b) returns the remainder when a is divided by b. Integer division (a // b) returns the quotient, discarding any fractional part. These are fundamental arithmetic operations in programming. Intuition: For positive integers, a = b * (a // b) + (a % b), with 0 ≤ (a % b) < b. For example, 17 % 5 = 2 because 17 = 5*3 + 2. Modulo is key in algorithms like Euclidean GCD for reducing numbers. Integer division is used in LCM formula to compute a*b/GCD without floating-point errors. Algorithm: In code, use % for modulo and // for integer division. Time complexity: O(1) for fixed-size integers. Space: O(1). Common mistakes: confusion with negative numbers (modulo behavior varies by language; in Python, a % b always returns non-negative result less than b, matching mathematical definition), integer division rounding toward negative infinity in Python 3 (// floors), and division by zero errors. This basic operation is used in L3's Euclidean algorithm for computing remainders and in L2's LCM formula for division.

examples.trace: Step 1: Integer division: 17 // 5 = 3. Step 2: Modulo: 17 % 5 = 2. Check: 5*3 + 2 = 15+2=17.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Multiples and Divisibility
ladders.desc: Understand multiples of a number and divisibility rules.
ladders.explanation: A multiple of an integer a is any integer that can be expressed as a*k for some integer k. For example, multiples of 3 are 0, 3, 6, 9, ... (including 0). Divisibility: an integer b is divisible by a if b % a == 0, meaning b is a multiple of a. The intuition: Multiples form an arithmetic sequence with common difference a. In the context of recurring tasks, if a task repeats every a days, it occurs on days that are multiples of a (day 0, a, 2a, ...). When multiple tasks have periods a, b, c, they coincide on days that are common multiples of all periods. The smallest positive common multiple is the Least Common Multiple (LCM). This foundational concept explains why LCM gives the first coincidence after day 0. Algorithmically, to check if a number is a multiple of a, use modulo: if num % a == 0. Time complexity: O(1). Space: O(1). Common mistakes: forgetting that 0 is a multiple of every number (since 0 = a*0), confusion between multiples and factors (a is a factor of b if b is a multiple of a), and not considering negative multiples (though in this problem, days are non-negative). This foundation underpins L4's modulo operations and leads to understanding LCM in higher levels.

examples.input: Check if 15 is divisible by 3 and 5
examples.output: divisible by 3: yes, divisible by 5: yes
examples.trace: Step 1: Check 15 % 3 = 0, so yes divisible by 3. Step 2: Check 15 % 5 = 0, so yes divisible by 5.
